title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane)
code: cur = Math.Max(nums[i], cur + nums[i]);   // extend, or restart at nums[i]

frames.caption: Initialize best = cur = nums[0] = -2. The current subarray is just [-2].
frames.note: best is the high-water mark: the largest cur ever seen, i.e. the best subarray sum ending ANYWHERE so far. cur can rise and fall as prefixes reset, but best only ever climbs, so the running maximum is a faithful summary we never have to recompute. Initializing best = cur = nums[0] (never 0) is what makes all-negative inputs correct: the answer is then the largest single element, not a phantom empty subarray of sum 0.

frames.caption: i = 1: cur = max(1, -2 + 1 = -1) = 1 — RESET. Prefix was non-positive, so drop it and start a fresh subarray at index 1.
frames.note: cur = nums[i] is the RESET case: the running prefix had gone non-positive, so dragging it along would only shrink nums[i]. Math.Max(nums[i], cur + nums[i]) picks nums[i] alone, which is identical to throwing the old prefix away and starting a fresh subarray AT i. The greedy insight: a non-positive prefix can never help any future subarray, so discarding it is always safe — that single keep-or-reset decision per index is the whole algorithm.

frames.caption: best = max(-2, 1) = 1 — new high-water mark. Best window so far is indices 1..1.
frames.note: best is the high-water mark: the largest cur ever seen, i.e. the best subarray sum ending ANYWHERE so far. cur can rise and fall as prefixes reset, but best only ever climbs, so the running maximum is a faithful summary we never have to recompute. Initializing best = cur = nums[0] (never 0) is what makes all-negative inputs correct: the answer is then the largest single element, not a phantom empty subarray of sum 0.

frames.caption: i = 2: cur = max(-3, 1 + -3 = -2) = -2 — EXTEND. The running prefix still helps, so grow the subarray to cover indices 1..2.
frames.note: cur = cur + nums[i] is the EXTEND case: the running prefix was positive, so carrying it forward beats starting over (cur + nums[i] >= nums[i]). cur is exactly dp[i] = best subarray sum ending at i, the DP table collapsed to one variable. As long as the prefix helps, Kadane never resets — it just keeps accumulating, which is why one left-to-right pass with O(1) state replaces the O(n^2) "try every subarray" brute force.

frames.caption: best = max(1, -2) = 1 — no improvement, best stays 1 (window 1..1).
frames.note: best is the high-water mark: the largest cur ever seen, i.e. the best subarray sum ending ANYWHERE so far. cur can rise and fall as prefixes reset, but best only ever climbs, so the running maximum is a faithful summary we never have to recompute. Initializing best = cur = nums[0] (never 0) is what makes all-negative inputs correct: the answer is then the largest single element, not a phantom empty subarray of sum 0.

frames.caption: i = 3: cur = max(4, -2 + 4 = 2) = 4 — RESET. Prefix was non-positive, so drop it and start a fresh subarray at index 3.
frames.note: cur = nums[i] is the RESET case: the running prefix had gone non-positive, so dragging it along would only shrink nums[i]. Initializing best = cur = nums[0] (never 0) is what makes all-negative inputs correct: the answer is then the largest single element, not a phantom empty subarray of sum 0.

frames.caption: Loop done. Return best = 6 — the maximum subarray is indices 3..6 = [4, -1, 2, 1].
frames.note: One pass, two scalars: cur made a local keep-or-reset decision at every index while best recorded the high-water mark — so the contiguous subarray with the largest sum falls out in O(n) time and O(1) space. This is Kadane as a DP in disguise: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1] + nums[i]) with the whole table collapsed to one running variable.
